content.abstract_or_snippet: 0.0552 m. However, under signal interference conditions, the system’s performance degrades significantly. The AM signal interference increases the mean error to 4.9484 m and the stan- dard deviation to 4.0833 m, with the elevation direction showing the most significant fluctuations. This is likely due to the AM signal inter- ference directly affecting signal power through amplitude variations, leading to enhanced vertical errors. The FM signal interference causes even more severe impacts, with the mean error rising to 6.2241 m and the standard deviation of 5.2630 m. The FM signal interference in- troduces frequency shifts exacerbating Doppler effects and significantly increasing error fluctuations in the eastward and northward directions. The pulse signal interference characterized by periodic bursts of high- intensity disruption, results in a mean error of 4.7925 m and a standard deviation of 3.6242 m. Despite a lower mean error compared to the FM signal interference, the pulse signal interference significantly affects all three spatial dimensions. This is due to its transient and repetitive nature which destabilizes signal reception and disrupts GNSS compu- tations. These findings demonstrate that different types of signal in- terferences uniquely affect GNSS-based positioning accuracy. The FM signal interference exerts the most severe impact followed by pulse and AM signal interference. The elevation direction is the most sensitive to such disruptions, highlighting the direct influence of signal interference characteristics on positioning stability in different directions. 4.2. Positioning performances under different environment scenarios Table 5 provides a detailed analysis of the performances of the po- sitioning system under three distinct environmental scenarios: Open Area, Mountain Occlusion, and Tunnel. Key metrics of mean error, standard deviation, and UTM coordinate system components are com- pared to assess the accuracy and stability of the system. This compar- ison highlights the impact of different environmental conditions on positioning performances. In open areas, it exhibits high precision with a mean error of 1.0279 m and minimal fluctuation, highlighting its robustness under unobstructed conditions. However, in mountainous environments, the mean error increases to 1.2979 m, reflecting the effects of signal mul- tipath and obstructions caused by complex terrain, with the elevation direction being particularly sensitive to these factors. GNSS signal, as a typical electromagnetic wave signal, can effectively determine the channel parameters of LOS signal and NLOS signal through raytracing. As shown in Fig. 6, the sender (TX) emits the signal, the trajectory is re- flected by the surrounding object, the receiver (RX) signal has second-order- reflection, thus as the NLOS signal. In the defined environment, the environmental data of the target sce- nario is mapped using LiDAR, 3D modeling is conducted by the method of UAV oblique photography. Since the environmental parameters, satellite ephemeris and trajectory simulation time are pre-defined conditions or in- formation, the channel model of the satellite signal is a deterministic channel, in which the electromagnetic wave propagation theory can be used to derive the electromagnetic wave channel. updated_hypotheses.statement: In Bahn-/Korridorszenarien führen in-band Interferenzsignale (AM, FM, pulsartig) zu einem sprunghaften Anstieg der GNSS-Positionsfehler von ca. ~1 m (Open Area) auf mehrere Meter; FM-Interferenz verursacht dabei im Mittel die größten Fehler und die stärkste Streuung (≈6.22 m Mittelwert, ≈5.26 m Standardabweichung) im Vergleich zu AM (≈4.95 m, ≈4.08 m) und Puls (≈4.79 m, ≈3.62 m).
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Sehr guter Fit: Dokumentiert sind quantitative Mittelwerte/Standardabweichungen der Positionsfehler unter AM/FM/Puls-Interferenz (AM 4.9484±4.0833 m; FM 6.2241±5.2630 m; Puls 4.7925±3.6242 m) sowie die Aussage, dass FM den stärksten Effekt hat; Open-Area ist deutlich präziser. Kein Gegenbeleg in bereitgestellten Dokumenten; verbleibende Einschränkung: Werte stammen aus CPN/EKF-Simulation und sind szenario-/parametrisierungsspezifisch.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Tunnel- und Portal-/Übergangsbereiche verursachen die stärkste Degradation der GNSS-Zugpositionierung: Im Tunnel kommt es zu vollständigem Signalausfall; direkt nach dem Portal entstehen durch Reacquisition unter Multipath/NLOS initial sehr große Fehler, die erst mit zunehmender Entfernung vom Portal wieder abklingen; im Modell liegt der mittlere Fehler im Tunnelszenario bei etwa 5.67 m (bei gleichzeitiger Phase ohne Positionierung im Tunnel).
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Guter Fit: Tunnelmodell beschreibt vollständige GNSS-Blockage im Tunnel, sehr große Fehler direkt nach dem Portal wegen Reacquisition unter Multipath/NLOS und anschließendes Abklingen; mittlerer Fehler 5.6670 m wird explizit genannt. Keine Gegenbelege in den Dokumenten; Limitation: Kennzahl ist an CPN-Szenario gebunden und beschreibt Mittelwert über Phasen inkl. Ausfallrepräsentation.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Bergiges Gelände verschlechtert GNSS-Genauigkeit im Bahnkorridor primär über deterministische Sichtbarkeitsselektion (Terrain-Occlusion): Satelliten werden aus der Positionslösung gefiltert, wenn ihr Elevationswinkel unter einem aus Berg-/Hindernishöhe und Distanz abgeleiteten Obstruktionswinkel liegt; dies erhöht den mittleren Positionsfehler gegenüber Open-Area messbar (z.B. von ≈1.03 m auf ≈1.30 m).
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Teilweise guter Fit: Der deterministische Sichtbarkeitsfilter (Elevationswinkel vs. aus Hindernishöhe/Distanz abgeleiteter Obstruktionswinkel) ist klar beschrieben. Die konkrete Fehlerdifferenz (≈1.03 m→≈1.30 m) ist in den bereitgestellten Snippets nicht direkt belegt; es ist nur qualitativ/narrativ, dass Occlusion Satelliten entfernt und Genauigkeit verschlechtert. Daher Evidenzbasis für die numerischen Werte zu schmal.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Die mathematische Bewertung der GNSS-Positionierungsqualität im Bahnkontext lässt sich operational über Integrity-Metriken (HPE, HPL, HAL, TTA sowie Pfa/Pmd) durchführen: Ein Alarm ist auszulösen, wenn HPL>HAL; Integritätszustände (Normal, SD, DD, DU, SU) ergeben sich aus den Ungleichungen zwischen HPE, HPL und HAL und erlauben eine sicherheitsgerichtete Klassifikation der Positionslösung über die reine Genauigkeit hinaus.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Guter Fit: Dokumente definieren Integrity-Parameter (HPE, HPL, HAL, TTA, Pfa, Pmd) und Entscheidungslogik: Alarm bei HPL>HAL; Zustandsklassifikation (Normal, DD, SD, DU, SU) per Ungleichungen zwischen HPE/HPL/HAL ist explizit beschrieben. Kein Gegenbeleg in bereitgestellten Quellen.

research_plan.reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf GNSS-Positionsgenauigkeit unter Signalstörungen (Multipath, Abschattung, Interferenz/Jamming/Spoofing, atmosphärische Effekte) und spezifischen Gelände-/Infrastrukturprofilen (Berge, Einschnitte, Tunnel/Portalbereiche) ab; im Schienenkontext sind dafür besonders Szenarien entlang von Korridoren mit hoher Abschattung und sicherheitsrelevante Qualitätsmaße (z.B. Integrity, Protection Levels) sowie mathematische Bewertungsmodelle (DOP, Fehlerbudget, stochastische Modelle, Ray-Tracing/3D-Modelle) relevant. Die Suchaufträge decken (1) Störungsarten und deren quantifizierte Effekte, (2) Geländemodellierung/Line-of-Sight und Tunnel-Übergänge, (3) mathematische Bewertungsmetriken inkl. Integrity, (4) Messkampagnen/Datensätze im Rail-Umfeld und (5) Normen/Leitfäden für GNSS in sicherheitskritischen Bahn-Anwendungen ab.

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 175–184 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets Shuting Chena,b, Daohua Wua,b,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b National Research Center of Railway Safety Assessment, Collaborative Innovation Center of Railway Traffic Safety, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Jiang Liua,c,d, Siqi Wanga,d ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS-based train positioning Positioning performance Environment scenario GNSS signal interference Colored petri Nets Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-based continuous and accurate train positioning is one of the key technologies for advanced train operations such as train virtual coupling. However, GNSS-based train posi- tioning faces significant challenges in real-world scenarios due to environmental complexities and signal in- terferences. Considering this issue, this paper presents an approach for modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning systems using Colored Petri Nets (CPNs). By systematically modeling the GNSS signal reception and processing process, the performance of the positioning system under various environment scenarios is evaluated. The system model integrates three types of interference signals (i.e., Amplitude Modulation (AM) signals, Frequency Modulation (FM) signals, and pulse signals) while incorporating environ- mental factors such as terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding. Additionally, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) algorithm is employed to process GNSS observation data, providing accurate train position estimations. The simulation results demonstrate that signal interferences and complex environmental conditions significantly affect the GNSS-based positioning accuracy. This study offers a comprehensive framework for evaluating the performance of GNSS-based train positioning systems in different scenarios, highlighting critical factors that influence positioning accuracy and stability. 1. Introduction With the increasing demand for operation safety and efficiency in rail transportation, train virtual coupling has been proposed and be- come a hot research topic in recent years. For virtual coupling

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways
content.abstract_or_snippet: atability within the simulated environment, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of zero on-site testing for GNSS-based train control systems. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways. 1. Introduction Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) have seen vast applications in the transportation domain. The integration of Global Positioning System (GPS) and Beidou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) in railway is efficient for modernizing the railway network, GNSS is increasingly becoming a fundamental pillar not only for non-safety applications, but also for im- proved maintenance, automated train operation, and safety- related appli- cations, including the Next Generation of Train Control (NGTC) and re- novation of the train on-board equipment, all these will increase the railway system capacity, efficiency and safety of all railways [1]. To apply GNSS in railway train operation, the GNSS performance testing and verification is the essential procedure for the system to be adopted and further listed as mandatory in the system design and development. The certification of the GNSS-based train localization systems is also necessary to provide concrete validation or testing application services to carry out simulation tests with evaluation of the platform and environmental condi- tion simulations [2]. Beidou has a set of technical and regulatory standards, including the BDS Performance Standard, BDS Signal Interface Control Document (ICD), and technical requirements for user equipment. The BDS Signal In Space (SIS) and service performance characteristics include ac- curacy, availability, compatibility, and interoperability [3]. GPS has also published standard positioning service performance standards to guide the user for civil uses worldwide, the performance metrics are GPS SIS accuracy, continuity, and availability [4]. So both BDS and GPS are focusing on ac- curacy as the foundation for the GNSS performance [5]. The performance needs to be assessed and as the performances are all credited to the equipment, the equipment itself needs to be certified. The certification procedure, it is governed by various international and regional standards, such as the accuracy standards by BDS and GPS, the test and simulation standard procedures, the simulation must meet possible (as sensible to the GNSS) to make the test results plausible. content.abstract_or_snippet: Railway 3 (2025) 116–124 and used the boundary to finally get the constrained state estimation problem. Liso Nicolás [13] used ray tracing to describe the channel characteristics between the satellite and the ground receiver and ex- tracted information from it to design a more positive algorithm, redu- cing the influence of multipath effects on positioning. This paper introduces a zero on-site testing scheme for GNSS perfor- mance evaluation for railway train control system adoption. The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the problems and challenges in the methods for zero on-site testing, Section 3 introduces the key technol- ogies in the testing procedure. After that, Section 4 introduces the data collected using the zero on-site testing platform, and Section 5 delivers the conclusion of the testing performance and characteristics. 2) GNSS signal propagation: with the train movement, the GNSS signal propagation in the real world can be affected by the environment along the railway track. As the optimized signal to be propagated is the Line-Of-Sight (LOS) signal, due to the obstacles along the track, the GNSS signal might be reflected, refracted, and blocked. Thus, environmental modelling is the basis for simulation. There are still many challenges in addressing scientific problems. The main problems to be discussed in this paper is the environmental modelling forming the ray-tracing procedure, and the train movement that integrates the environment and train location to generate the ap- propriate GNSS signal for the zero on-site testing. 2. Problems description 3. Key technologies in zero on-site testing of GNSS Since the benefits of zero on-site testing have been illustrated, the state-of-the-art for simulation of GNSS has also been demonstrated, but there are still scientific problems and challenges to be addressed for implementing zero on-site testing. The zero on-site testing procedure shall be composed of the procedure starting with the test control and management, then the train movement and simulation. With the si- mulated train trajectory similar to Fig. 1, the GNSS signal simulation and the modeled 3D environment together can generate the GNSS signal by the GNSS simulator, the simulated GNSS signal propagation needs to be modeled in the simulator to evaluate the Non-Line-Of-Sight (NLOS) multipath effects estimations [14]. With the generated GNSS signal, the signal can either be coupled directly into the device under test through SMA or TNC interfaces or using the Over-The-Air (OTA) testing

content.abstract_or_snippet: . Safe train envelope estimation For GNSS-based train positioning and train integrity monitoring, the uncertainty in state estimation has to be concerned to ensure the safety and efficiency of train operation by utilizing the integrity monitoring result. Under the moving block train control scheme, where the occu- pancy of the track segments is determined by the practical length of the train, it is necessary to add a reasonable margin to the raw HOT and EOT location to form a safe occupancy range [13]. The safe train position has to cover the most unfavorable conditions. With this consideration, the margin should be sufficiently large to realize a high possibility to protect the unsatisfied situations. However, an excessive margin will lead to unreasonable identification of track occupancy, which may greatly af- fect the operational efficiency. To realize the expected balance level between the safety and efficiency characteristics, a constrained safe margin has to be adopted on the basis of safety and rationality, so as to reduce the train tracing interval and improve the system efficiency. The essence of the safe train envelope is to realize the safety as- surance of train control by describing the reliability of train positioning. The safe train envelope extends the positioning output based on the evaluation of the uncertainty of positioning errors, and provides a safe overlay to the most possible position derived by the HOT/EOT positioning unit. The safe margin consists of the HPL by integrity monitoring result and the confidential interval of the positioning error. The HPL, which indicates the radius of a circle in the horizontal plane with its center being at the true position to describe the region assured to contain the indicated horizontal position, is usually regarded as the safety boundary of positioning result by GNSS. However, the existence of the integrity risk may lead to an unbounded position error that exceeds the HPL. Without the high-level referencing system during the dynamic operation, it is difficult to accurately evaluate the relia- bility of the safety boundary by HPL. In order to guarantee the cred- ibility of the safe margin for envelope evaluation, it is necessary to estimate the possible range of the positioning error by a specific error confidence interval strategy. Considering the track-constrained along- track operation of railway trains, the error confidence interval indicates a one-dimensional along-track interval that can be represented by the uncertainties in state prediction, measurement update and the trackmap-based location determination, which means (4) and where confidence interval at instant k, result, geographic information from the trackmap database. represent the lower and upper limits of the indicates the user measurement represents the prior denotes the prediction result, and

Virtual Balise The concept of VB (Virtual Balise) can effectively integrate the sa- tellite positioning technology into existing train control system frame- works without modification to the specifications [7,8]. VB capture is acknowledged by matching the train position and the preset VB re- ference position along the track [9]. Within a specific distance limit, a success matching will enable the determination of the time instant when a train passes through the target VB. Then, the VB telegram can be ex- tracted and transmitted to the ATP (Automatic Train Protection) system kernel. Based on the existing VB capture mechanism, a pre-capture de- cision logic is necessary to effectively reduce the risk of missed VB capturing. A VB capture recognition method based on the forward search strategy is proposed to accurately determine the position and time of VB capture, which improves the VB capture performance over conventional solution requires a pre-defined capture interval. 3.1. Pre-capture decision logic With the aid of the train state prediction method, the NVB (Next Virtual Balise) pre-capture decision is made according to the relative position relationship between the train and the NVB. The evaluation of the integrity status is different from the accuracy since it concerns more about the behaviors of the perception logic against the critical conditions with fault(s) or failure(s). It can be de- scribed by the false alarm rate, missed detection rate, HPE, HPL, HAL and TTA (Time to Alert), which are summarized in Table 3. For the train positioning integrity assessment aiming at train posi- tioning status identification, the output parameter is the train posi- tioning output result integrity status. In the train positioning integrity state, in addition to the normal state, there are three non-normal states: alarmed non-hazardous state, alarmed hazardous state and no alarmed hazardous state. For the integrity assessment aiming at the identification of train positioning status, the “integrity state” will be identified as the output of the assessment, which is defined as status of the train positioning output results obtained with the integrity assessment input parameters. It can be classified as normal, safe alarmed, dangerous alarmed, and dangerous un-alarmed. 3) Availability. According to the definition of availability in EN50126, availability of an integrated train positioning system like GNSS/INS mode is defined as “the extent to which the train positioning is available at any given moment”. The required parameters for the availability assessment mainly include the integrity status, hardware failure rate, and environmental scene parameters, which can be de- scribed as Table 4. The availability evaluation results can be reflected by the in- stantaneous availability and steady-state availability. The instantaneous availability A(t) evaluates the probability that a train positioning unit normally provides the reliable location service at a certain moment. Number of in-view satellites is up to 11. The scenario maintained a sustained velocity between 30 and 35 m/s throughout the trajectory. Scenario II: The trajectory starts at GPS week 2034, second of week 289800, lasting for 160 s. The trajectory also replicated a train journey along the Qinghai–Xizang Railway from Nagqu to Amdo section. A number of in-view satellites is up to 5. The velocity continuously ac- celerated from 25 to 27 m/s. In the simulated test, a GNSS signal at GPS L1 frequency is simulated with a reference level of −130 dBm, while the jamming generator operates at a center frequency of 1575.42 MHz with a power level of −90 dBm. The Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR), i.e., the ratio of the jamming signal power to the GNSS signal power, is used to express the relationship between GNSS signal power and jamming power. Beyond complete signal obstruction scenarios, intermediate jamming power levels can induce significant performance degradation in GNSS re- ceivers without fully blocking satellite signal acquisition. These inter- mediate power levels would be hazardous and difficult to detect and mitigate, making it crucial to accurately assess the impact of jamming on GNSS signal quality. Previous research [18] has demonstrated sub- stantial signal quality degradation under jamming conditions with JSR values ranging from 38 to 54 dB. Accordingly, this study focuses on a 40 dB JSR scenario, corresponding to the specified −130 dBm GPS signal and −90 dBm jamming power configuration. A 5 Hz ramp waveform is employed, sweeping ± 1 kHz around the carrier. The jamming signal for investigating the collected IF signal, and the software-defined radio GNSS receiver are used to analyze the data. This software receiver is configured with a second-order phase-locked loop and a second-order code delay lock loop. The carrier loop band- width is set to 3 Hz, while the code loop bandwidth is maintained at 3 Hz. In the proposed solution, INS mechanization updates every 1 ms, while the tracking loop

content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning Zhuojian Caoa, Jiang Liua,b,c,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Wei Jianga,b,c, Baigen Caia,b,c ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS jamming Deeply-coupled integration GNSS/INS Railway safety and efficiency increasingly rely on precise train positioning. The integration of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) into railway control systems aims to reduce dependence on track-side in- frastructure. While GNSS has significantly improved train localization, challenges such as the susceptibility to jamming remain. To address this, this paper introduces an Inertial Navigation System (INS)-aided train posi- tioning system based on deep integration, exploring its performance through semi-physical experiments and simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution is able to reduce the positioning error by 63.47 %, and the velocity error by 58.47 % under jamming conditions. The study highlights the potential of deep integration for improving the resilience of GNSS-based train control systems, especially in the face of Radio Frequency (RF) jamming threats. 1. Introduction Railway safety and efficiency depend increasingly on knowing every train’s position. Traditional train positioning technologies usually in- volve track circuit positioning, axle counter positioning, and intersec- tion inductive loop positioning. However, these methods are often limited by insufficient positioning accuracy and high maintenance costs. The application of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) in railway train control systems aims to reduce as much as possible the use of track-side infrastructures for train location awareness, including track circuits and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Early in the 1990s, the United States introduces the concept of the

High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 information from both the INS and GNSS pseudorange/pseudorange rate, where a specific estimator (e.g., nonlinear Kalman filter) would be adopted. Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra- tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. [23–25], based on the time-frequency behavior, jamming signals can be typically divided into several classes, including Continuous Wave (CW), AM, FM, Frequency Sweep (FS; also referred to is the sampling interval, and as chirp jamming), Narrow-Band Gaussian white Noise (NBGN), and Wideband Gaussian white Noise (WBGN). It is found in Ref. [25] that the majority of the jammers use chirp signals, which vary the signal frequency linearly over time . The chirp signal exhibits a constant rate of frequency change over time, within a certain frequency range and sweep period. This sweep procedure is characterized by the initial and frequency , and the sweep period T. Thus, the instantaneous frequency is , the minimum and maximum frequency at given by The time-domain representation of the chirp signal is modeled as (2) (3) The jamming signals will not only disrupt the proper functioning of the receiver’s RF front-end components, such as low-noise amplifiers, automatic gain control, and analog-to-digital converters, but also ad- versely affect the operation of the carrier and code tracking loops. This jamming can prevent the receiver from acquiring and maintaining a stable lock on the satellite signals, ultimately leading to degraded po- sitioning performance. 3.2. INS-aided jamming protection solution Fig. 2 illustrates the INS-aided GNSS tracking loop. The carrier signal measurement enters the tracking loop through the phase dis- criminator to generate a phase error estimate. This phase error drives the carrier loop filter which outputs an estimate of the signal Doppler to drive the carrier controller oscillator (NCO). The NCO duplicates the phase of the incoming signal, and this phase estimate is fed back to the phase discriminator. The tracking loop is usually said to be “locked” if the phase error is less than five degrees. Jiang et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 It is of great importance to develop specific countermeasures to protect train positioning from jamming-caused GNSS disturbance. Research institutions and GNSS receiver manufacturers have been developing GNSS receivers equipped with anti-jamming capabilities. A survey of state-of-art approaches is introduced, based on the functional stages of the receiver in which they operate. Typically, they could be implemented at the front-end, pre-correction, or post-correlation stages, with others functioning at the navigation processing stage. Spatial fil- tering [9–11], functioning at the front-end stage, uses antenna arrays to point the receiver antenna beam toward the GNSS satellites and away from jammers. In the pre-correction stage, time/frequency filtering [12–14] enables the jamming mitigation before the jamming signal enters the receiver. Vector tracking is a typical approach that works on the post-correction stage. Instead of tracking in-view satellites sepa- rately in the conventional baseband signal processing, vector tracking [15,16] is achieved through feedback of the receiver states from the navigation module to the signal tracking channels as prior information and constraints. Additionally, the inertial-aiding-based strategy at the navigation processing stage provides a different way. INS is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. Overall, these results highlight the effectiveness of INS-aided solution in improving the accuracy and stability of both positioning and velocity estimation. The integration of inertial sensors helps to minimize errors caused by jamming, thereby enhancing the overall performance of the naviga- tion system. 191 Z. Cao, J. Liu, W. Jiang et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 Fig. 8. The positioning errors and the velocity errors for the normal tracking solution and the INS-aided solution in scenario II. 5. Conclusion In this paper, a GNSS jamming protection solution is presented based on the INS-aided deep integration solution. The solution enables a self-contained PVT reference that is immune to RF signals. INS in- formation is sent to the receiver’s baseband, realizing the assistance to the receiver at the signal processing level. The measured velocity in- formation from INS is mapped to the LOS between the satellite and the vehicle to obtain the Doppler of relative motion. The Doppler is fed back to the NCO in the tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The the proposed solution demonstrates significant evaluation of improvements in positioning and velocity, enabling resilient train po- sitioning for securing next generation GNSS-based train control sys- tems. Future work will focus on refining mitigation techniques with extended studies for multi-constellation scenarios (e.g., BeiDou and Galileo), and exploring field tests in real lines to ensure the resilience of GNSS-based train localization systems in railway applications. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: terrain, with the elevation direction being particularly sensitive to these factors. Tunnel scenarios show the most pronounced degradation with a mean error of 5.6670 m. This dramatic increase results from severe signal blockages in tunnels and the instability during signal reacquisition after exiting the tunnel. Fig. 10 illustrates the evolution of position error in the tunnel en- vironment. During the “Inside the Tunnel” phase, GNSS signals are completely blocked by the surrounding structure, resulting in a loss of positioning capability (represented by the gray shaded region). Upon exiting the tunnel (marked by the vertical dashed line), the system begins to reacquire GNSS signals; however, due to severe multipath effects and signal obstructions, the initial position errors are sig- nificantly large. As the train moves further away from the tunnel exit, the surrounding environment improves, leading to a gradual reduction in error, which eventually stabilizes. This trend highlights the critical Fig. 10. Position errors in tunnel scenario. impact of tunnel environments and their transitions on GNSS-based positioning performance. 5. Conclusion This paper presents a CPNs-based approach to evaluate the perfor- mances of GNSS-based train positioning systems under real-world op- erational conditions. By constructing a hierarchical CPN model, the study successfully models the process of GNSS signal reception and interferences, as well as the impacts of complex environmental factors on the GNSS-based train positioning. The simulation results emphasize that signal interferences (i.e., interfered by AM, FM, and pulse signals) have a significant impact on the GNSS-based positioning accuracy, with FM interference exerting the most pronounced effects, particularly in the vertical direction. Environmental factors like terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding further degrade signal visibility and increase po- sitioning errors. The model also identifies critical challenges during signal reacquisition, particularly in tunnel transitions, which exacerbate error growth. While the proposed approach effectively demonstrates the impact of various signal interferences and environmental conditions on the per- formance of the GNSS-based train positioning, future research could explore the integration of additional signal interference mitigation techniques to improve the accuracy and stability of the GNSS-based positioning for railway applications. content.abstract_or_snippet: . S. Chen, D. Wu, J. Liu et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 175–184 Fig. 5. Submodule Open Area. to filter out signals from satellites that are obstructed by the terrain. The places Mountain Height and Distance store mountain elevations and spatial relationships between the terrain and GNSS signal paths. These serve as the foundational data for modeling and analyzing how terrain features obstruct satellite signals. The transition Mountain Data Fusion and its code are responsible for integrating and transmitting these data. The place GNSS Signal stores the initial GNSS satellite data. The transition Mountain Scenario integrates mountain height and obstruc- tion distance information with current visible satellite data to filter out obstructed signals. This process assesses satellite visibility by com- paring each satellite’s elevation angle with the obstruction angle de- rived from the mountain height and the receiver’s distance to the mountain. If a satellite’s elevation angle is lower than the obstruction angle, it is deemed as obstructed and excluded from the positioning calculations. After the firing of the transition Mountain Scenario, the GNSS data are stored in the place Data with Mountain Error. Additionally, visible satellite data are combined with interference sig- nals and output to the place GNSS Observation. By incorporating a mountainous terrain scenario, this module effectively simulates the dynamic impact of terrain obstructions on GNSS signal visibility in complex environments. 3.2.3. Submodule Tunnel Fig. 7 illustrates the process of GNSS signal reception and error variation for a train in a tunnel scenario. It focuses on the dynamic impact of the tunnel environment on the satellite signal visibility and positioning accu- racy. In this module, the places InTunnel, Just Out, and Out Tunnel re- present the train’s current state, including three phases: inside the tunnel, just outside the tunnel, and completely outside the tunnel. Inside the tunnel, a train is shielded by the structure blocking GNSS signal reception. During this phase, the place GNSS Signal remains empty indicating the complete obstruction of satellite sig- nals. When the train just exits the tunnel, the GNSS signals become visible again. However, due to the complex environmental condi- tions near the tunnel exit, such as multipath effects, signal reflec- tions, and delays, the GNSS observation data contain significant er- rors. This process is modeled by the code of the transition Tunnel Scenario, which integrates the tunnel state from the place Tunnel with visible satellite information.

Conclusion This paper introduces a zero on-site testing platform on GNSS for train control towards digital railways, the platform utilizes the GNSS RF signal to be broadcasted through the multiplier of both GNSS signal and multipath signal, which enables the receiver to operate in an environ- ment where the signal reception is identical to the physical world. The proposed methods in this paper train dynamic modeling, 3D environ- ment modeling with digital track map, and the ray-tracing techniques. With the proposed method and the setup of the zero on-site platform, the tests on open area and cutting environment are tested, and the test results demonstrate the zero on-site ability of the platform. Further, repeatability and reproducibility need to be performed for the accreditation of the platform for the adoption of the certification process for the GNSS in railway applications. Zero on-site testing platform The test management module controls the test process with the test case and test sequence for the simulator control; the train dynamics and environment generation module generates the train movement, and the train movement is converted from mileage to the WGS-84 latitude and longitude information using the digital track map; then the signal generation module generates the BDS/GPS signal together with the multipath signal, all the signals are generated into the device under test in the anechoic chamber; finally, the evaluation module performs the data management and using the evaluation algorithm to go through the evaluation and display the 3D ray-tracing process. The hardware of the platform is shown in Fig. 14. 4.3. Simulation results and data analysis According to the simulation platform architecture, the train move- ment is defined using the high-speed railway CRH380AL. The train parameters are used to generate the train movement trajectory with the DTM and time information. Then the trajectory is converted to the WGS-84 coordinate for simulation (Fig. 15). Since the train is moving on the Test Base of China Academy of Railway Sciences (Circular Railway), the train can work continuously on the ring, which benefits for as long as requested tests. The open area and cutting (urban canyon) are investigated. For the open area environment, the test case was carried out in the environment 4 times. Over time, as the GNSS signal re- ception stabilizes, the positioning errors gradually decrease. This dynamic adjustment is governed by the place Time Counter. The place GNSS Observation outputs the GNSS data affected by the tunnel scenario, including both the high-error initial data and the stabilized high-accuracy data. This module effectively captures the three-phase impact of tunnel scenarios on GNSS signal reception: complete signal loss inside the tunnel, significant errors immediately after exiting, and eventual signal stabilization outside the tunnel. 3.3. Module GNSS Position Solution Fig. 8 illustrates the process of transforming GNSS observations into train positions. GNSS observations, received from the GNSS Receiver module, are sequentially processed by the transition EKF. The com- pletion of the EKF process is indicated by the output flag bEKF=true, which signals readiness for subsequent evaluation. Due to the limited matrix computation capability of CPN, the tokens on the place Position are generated with MATLAB. content.abstract_or_snippet: 0.0552 m. However, under signal interference conditions, the system’s performance degrades significantly. The AM signal interference increases the mean error to 4.9484 m and the stan- dard deviation to 4.0833 m, with the elevation direction showing the most significant fluctuations. This is likely due to the AM signal inter- ference directly affecting signal power through amplitude variations, leading to enhanced vertical errors. The FM signal interference causes even more severe impacts, with the mean error rising to 6.2241 m and the standard deviation of 5.2630 m. The FM signal interference in- troduces frequency shifts exacerbating Doppler effects and significantly increasing error fluctuations in the eastward and northward directions. The pulse signal interference characterized by periodic bursts of high- intensity disruption, results in a mean error of 4.7925 m and a standard deviation of 3.6242 m. Despite a lower mean error compared to the FM signal interference, the pulse signal interference significantly affects all three spatial dimensions. This is due to its transient and repetitive nature which destabilizes signal reception and disrupts GNSS compu- tations. These findings demonstrate that different types of signal in- terferences uniquely affect GNSS-based positioning accuracy. The FM signal interference exerts the most severe impact followed by pulse and AM signal interference. The elevation direction is the most sensitive to such disruptions, highlighting the direct influence of signal interference characteristics on positioning stability in different directions. 4.2. Positioning performances under different environment scenarios Table 5 provides a detailed analysis of the performances of the po- sitioning system under three distinct environmental scenarios: Open Area, Mountain Occlusion, and Tunnel. Key metrics of mean error, standard deviation, and UTM coordinate system components are com- pared to assess the accuracy and stability of the system. This compar- ison highlights the impact of different environmental conditions on positioning performances. In open areas, it exhibits high precision with a mean error of 1.0279 m and minimal fluctuation, highlighting its robustness under unobstructed conditions. However, in mountainous environments, the mean error increases to 1.2979 m, reflecting the effects of signal mul- tipath and obstructions caused by complex terrain, with the elevation direction being particularly sensitive to these factors. synthesis_output.answer:
## Kurzfazit
Verschiedene Signalstörungen (AM/FM/Puls-Interferenz, Jamming) und Gelände-/Infrastrukturprofile (Einschnitte/Cutting, bergige Abschattung, Tunnel inkl. Portalbereiche) verschlechtern die GNSS-Positionierungsgenauigkeit im Bahnkorridor über zwei Hauptmechanismen: (1) Messfehlerzuwachs in den GNSS-Beobachtungen (z. B. durch Interferenz/Jamming oder Multipath) und (2) Verschlechterung der Satellitengeometrie bzw. vollständiger Sichtbarkeitsverlust (z. B. durch Terrain-Masking oder Tunnelabschattung). Quantitativ zeigen die vorliegenden Studien Fehleranstiege von etwa ~1 m in offenen Bereichen auf mehrere Meter unter Interferenz und in Tunnel-/Übergangsbereichen; mathematisch lässt sich der Einfluss über Fehlermaße (Positionsfehler, Mittelwert/Standardabweichung, Fehlerellipse) sowie sicherheitsgerichtet über Integritätsmetriken (HPE/HPL/HAL, Pfa/Pmd, TTA und Zustandslogik) bewerten. [1][2][3][4][5]

## Relevante Störungsarten und ihre Wirkmechanismen
### In-band Interferenz (AM, FM, Puls)
Ein CPN/EKF-basierter Simulationsrahmen integriert explizit drei Interferenzarten (AM, FM, Puls) zur Bewertung der Positionierungsleistung in unterschiedlichen Umweltszenarien. [1][6]
- AM-Interferenz wirkt über Amplitudenvariationen und beeinflusst damit die Signalstärke; dies wird in den Ergebnissen besonders mit starken Schwankungen in der Höhen-/Elevationskomponente in Verbindung gebracht. [2]
- FM-Interferenz führt zu Frequenzverschiebungen und verstärkt Doppler-bezogene Effekte; sie erhöht die Fehlerfluktuationen vor allem in Ost-/Nordrichtung und zeigt insgesamt die stärkste Degradation. [2]
- Puls-Interferenz (periodische Bursts hoher Intensität) destabilisiert transient die Signalaufnahme und wirkt auf alle drei Raumdimensionen. [2]

### Intentionales oder unbeabsichtigtes Jamming
GNSS-Signale liegen typischerweise sehr schwach unterhalb des thermischen Rauschbodens (z. B. GPS-Signalpegel typischerweise um −127 dBm), weshalb Jamming das Tracking (Code-/Trägerschleifen) stören und dadurch die PVT-Lösung degradieren oder ausfallen lassen kann. [7][8]
Jamming wird u. a. anhand des Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR) bewertet; eine Studie fokussiert explizit ein 40 dB-JSR-Setup (−130 dBm GNSS-Signal, −90 dBm Jammer) und verweist darauf, dass bereits „intermediate“ Jamming-Level ohne vollständige Blockade eine signifikante, schwer detektierbare Degradation verursachen können. [9]
Jamming-Signale lassen sich nach Zeit-Frequenz-Verhalten u. a. in CW, AM, FM, Frequency Sweep (Chirp), Narrow-/Wideband Gaussian Noise klassifizieren; als in der Praxis verbreitet werden insbesondere Chirp-/Sweep-Signale genannt. [10]

## Einfluss spezifischer Gelände- und Infrastrukturprofile
### Bergiges Gelände / Terrain-Occlusion (Satellitensichtbarkeit)
Im CPN-Modell wird bergige Abschattung als deterministischer Sichtbarkeitsfilter modelliert: Aus Berg-/Hindernishöhe und Distanz wird ein Obstruktionswinkel abgeleitet und Satelliten werden aus der Positionslösung ausgeschlossen, wenn ihr Elevationswinkel unter diesem Obstruktionswinkel liegt. [11]
Dieses Vorgehen bildet mathematisch den Kernmechanismus von Terrain-Masking ab: weniger sichtbare Satelliten und veränderte Geometrie erhöhen typischerweise die Positionsunsicherheit (in den Ergebnissen wird für bergige Szenarien eine Verschlechterung gegenüber Open Area berichtet). [2][11]

### Einschnitt/Cutting (urbane Schlucht) und geometrische Degradation
Für eine „cutting environment“ (im Text auch als urban canyon eingeordnet) werden deutlich erhöhte horizontale Fehler berichtet. Die Autoren führen dies auf Abschattungen und Multipath zurück, die die Zahl sichtbarer Satelliten reduzieren und „bad geometries“ verursachen. [12]

### Tunnel und Portal-/Übergangsbereiche
Tunnel werden als Extremfall der Abschattung beschrieben:
- Im Tunnel selbst ist der GNSS-Empfang vollständig blockiert (keine Satellitensignale), wodurch die Positionierungsfähigkeit verloren geht. [11][3]
- Direkt nach dem Portal treten während der Signal-Reacquisition zunächst große Fehler auf, bedingt durch Multipath, Reflexionen und Verzögerungen nahe dem Tunnelexit; mit zunehmender Entfernung stabilisiert sich der Empfang und die Fehler nehmen ab. [11][3]
Dieser Übergangsmechanismus wird als kritischer Treiber der Degradation hervorgehoben. [3]

## Quantitative Befunde zu Genauigkeitsdegradation (Rail-Korridor)
### Fehleranstieg unter AM/FM/Puls-Interferenz (Simulation)
Unter Interferenzbedingungen steigen die Fehlerkennwerte von „präzisen“ Bedingungen auf mehrere Meter; dabei ist FM am stärksten. In den berichteten Simulationsergebnissen:
- Open-Area-Basis: mittlerer Fehler 1,0279 m (mit geringer Fluktuation) [2]
- AM-Interferenz: mittlerer Fehler 4,9484 m; Standardabweichung 4,0833 m [2]
- FM-Interferenz: mittlerer Fehler 6,2241 m; Standardabweichung 5,2630 m [2]
- Puls-Interferenz: mittlerer Fehler 4,7925 m; Standardabweichung 3,6242 m [2]

### Fehler in bergigem Gelände und Tunnel (Simulation)
Für Umweltszenarien werden ebenfalls Mittelwerte berichtet:
- Bergige Abschattung: mittlerer Fehler 1,2979 m (u. a. durch Multipath und Obstruktionen; Elevation besonders sensitiv) [2]
- Tunnel: mittlerer Fehler 5,6670 m; begründet mit starken Blockagen und Instabilität der Reacquisition nach dem Tunnel [3]

### Mess-/Testbasierte Fehler in Open Area vs. Cutting (Ray-Tracing/Zero on-site Testing)
In einer „zero on-site testing“-Plattform mit 3D-Digital-Track-Map und Ray-Tracing werden horizontale Fehlerstatistiken berichtet:
- Open Area: Mittelwert 0,75 m; Standardabweichung 0,12 m; Maximum 0,97 m [12]
- Cutting: Mittelwert 2,03 m; Standardabweichung 1,09 m; Maximum 9,58 m [12]
Zusätzlich wird explizit festgehalten, dass Mittelwert und Standardabweichung im Cutting etwa dreimal so groß wie in Open Area sind, was auf Abschattung/Multi­path und damit reduzierte Satellitenzahl sowie ungünstige Geometrie zurückgeführt wird. [12]

## Mathematische Bewertung: von Fehlermaßen bis Integritätsnachweis
### 1) Direktes Genauigkeitsmaß: Positionsfehler, Statistik und 3D-Abstand
Ein verwendetes Bewertungsmaß ist der euklidische 3D-Abstand zwischen Referenzposition und berechneter Position:
- d = ||(x1,y1,z1) − (x2,y2,z2)||, wobei (x1,y1,z1) die Referenz und (x2,y2,z2) die berechnete Position darstellt. [5]
Für Szenariovergleiche werden daraus statistische Kenngrößen (Mittelwert, Standardabweichung) abgeleitet und zwischen Umwelten bzw. Störarten verglichen. [2][3]

### 2) Fehlerellipse als 2D-Horizontalmetrik (inkl. Kovarianz)
Für horizontale Performance wird die Fehlerellipse genutzt; die Berechnung umfasst Standardabweichungen in x/y sowie Kovarianz und eine anschließende Standardisierung gemäß Fehlerellipsengleichung sowie Winkelbestimmung zwischen Ellipsenachse und x-Achse. [13]
Die Fehlerellipse dient damit als kompakte geometrische Darstellung von Streuung/Unsicherheit im Horizontalraum (z. B. über „long axis/short axis“). [12][13]

### 3) Integritätsmetriken und sicherheitsgerichtete Entscheidungslogik (Rail)
Für sicherheitsrelevante Bahn-Anwendungen werden Accuracy und Integrity als getrennte, aber gekoppelte Bewertungsdimensionen beschrieben. Accuracy wird über Positionsfehler e und einen Vertrauensgrad (1−α) gefasst. [4]
Integrity wird für horizontale Lösungen u. a. mit folgenden Parametern operationalisiert: HPE, Pfa, Pmd, HPL, HAL, TTA (Time to Alert). [4][14]
Eine konkrete Entscheidungslogik klassifiziert Integritätszustände über Ungleichungen zwischen HPE, HPL und HAL (Normal, DD, SD, DU, SU) und spezifiziert u. a.:
- Alarmbedingung: Wenn HPL > HAL, soll ein Alarm ausgelöst werden. [15]
- Zustandsklassen (Auszug): Normal, wenn HAL > HPL > HPE; Dangerous Undetected (DU), wenn HPE > HAL > HPL; Safe Undetected (SU), wenn HAL > HPE > HPL; sowie No-GNSS-Output als separater Fall. [16][15]
Diese Logik erlaubt eine mathematisch eindeutige, sicherheitsgerichtete Bewertung auch dann, wenn reine Fehlerstatistiken (Mittelwert/Std) die Risikolage (z. B. unentdeckte gefährliche Fehler) nicht ausreichend abbilden. [15][16]

### 4) Verknüpfung zu Availability/RAMS
Im Bahnkontext wird Availability im Sinne von EN50126 als „extent to which … available at any given moment“ beschrieben; als Eingangsgrößen werden u. a. Integritätsstatus, Hardware-Failure-Rate und Environmental-Scene-Parameter genannt. [17][4]
Tunnel-„Inside“-Phasen mit vollständiger Blockage sind damit nicht nur ein Genauigkeits-, sondern auch ein Availability-Problem (keine GNSS-Ausgabe). [11][3][17]

## Modellierungsansätze zur quantitativen Bewertung (wie man Gelände/Störung in Gleichungen bekommt)
### Deterministische Sichtbarkeitsmodelle (Terrain)
Der beschriebenen Mountain-Ansatz operationalisiert Abschattung über einen Obstruktionswinkel, der aus Höhen-/Distanzinformationen abgeleitet und mit dem Satelliten-Elevationswinkel verglichen wird; damit wird Sichtbarkeit (und indirekt Geometrie/Beobachtungsset) deterministisch selektiert. [11]

### 3D-Umweltmodell + Ray-Tracing (Multipath/NLOS)
Ray-Tracing wird als Kanalmodellierung eingesetzt, um LOS- und NLOS-Anteile deterministisch aus einer vorab modellierten 3D-Umgebung (z. B. LiDAR/UAV-3D-Modell) abzuleiten; Reflexionspfade werden u. a. über Spiegelpunktkonstruktion (auch 2. Ordnung) bestimmt. [13]
Die „zero on-site testing“-Architektur kombiniert digitale Track-Map, 3D-Umweltmodell und Ray-Tracing, um GNSS-Signale inkl. Multipath zu erzeugen und die Empfängerperformance zu evaluieren. [18][12]

### Störungssignal-Modelle (Interferenz/Jamming)
- In-band Interferenzen werden als explizite Signaltypen (AM/FM/Puls) als schaltbare Störmodule in die Beobachtungsdatengenerierung eingebracht. [6]
- Für Jamming wird ein Signalmodell am ADC-Ausgang angegeben, das LOS-GNSS-Signal, demoduliertes Jamming und additives weißes Gaußrauschen umfasst; darüber werden Klassifikation und Parametrisierung (z. B. Chirp/Sweep mit Start-/Endfrequenz und Sweep-Periode) formalisiert. [10]

## Schlussfolgerungen für die Forschungsfrage
1) Signalstörungen erhöhen GNSS-Positionsfehler primär über eine Verschlechterung der Beobachtungsqualität (Tracking-/Messfehler), wobei FM-Interferenz im betrachteten Vergleich die größten mittleren Fehler und Streuungen erzeugt. [2]
2) Geländeprofile wirken über Sichtbarkeits- und Geometriemechanismen: Terrain-Masking filtert Satelliten (Elevations- vs. Obstruktionswinkel), Einschnitte reduzieren Sichtbarkeit und verschlechtern die Geometrie; Tunnel führen zu totalem Signalausfall und kritischen Reacquisition-Fehlern am Portal. [11][12][3]
3) Mathematisch lässt sich der Einfluss mehrstufig bewerten: (a) metrisch über 3D-Euklidfehler und dessen Statistik, (b) horizontal über Fehlerellipse/Kovarianz, und (c) sicherheitsgerichtet über Integritätsmetriken (HPE/HPL/HAL, Pfa/Pmd, TTA) und Zustandslogik inklusive Alarmbedingungen. [5][13][4][15][16]

Missing information:
- Explizite, quellengestützte Gleichungen zur direkten Verknüpfung von Satellitengeometrie (z. B. DOP) mit Positionsfehler/Kovarianz sind in den vorliegenden Auszügen nicht enthalten.
- Parametrisierung/Leistungskennwerte für Spoofing (gezielte Täuschung) fehlen vollständig in den bereitgestellten Quellen.
- Details, wie „No GNSS output“/vollständiger Ausfall innerhalb der Tunnelphase statistisch in den mittleren Fehler 5,6670 m eingeht (z. B. ob Ausfallzeiten aus der Statistik ausgeschlossen oder als Fehler kodiert werden), sind nicht vollständig spezifiziert.
- Quantitative Beziehungen zwischen spezifischen Terrainprofilparametern (Berg-/Einschnitthöhen, Öffnungswinkel, Tunnellänge) und Fehlerzuwachs (Sensitivitätskurven) werden nicht berichtet.
- Für Interferenz (AM/FM/Puls) fehlen in den Auszügen die verwendeten Leistungspegel/JSR und Empfängerparameter, die zur Generalisierung der Zahlen erforderlich wären.
- Normative Grenzwerte/Anforderungen (z. B. konkrete HAL/HPL-Zielwerte für ETCS/ERTMS-Anwendungsfälle) sind in den bereitgestellten Auszügen nicht enthalten.

Aufgestellte Hypothesen:
Hypothese 1: In Bahn-/Korridorszenarien führen in-band Interferenzsignale (AM, FM, pulsartig) zu einem sprunghaften Anstieg der GNSS-Positionsfehler von ca. ~1 m (Open Area) auf mehrere Meter; FM-Interferenz verursacht dabei im Mittel die größten Fehler und die stärkste Streuung (≈6.22 m Mittelwert, ≈5.26 m Standardabweichung) im Vergleich zu AM (≈4.95 m, ≈4.08 m) und Puls (≈4.79 m, ≈3.62 m).
Reasoning: fit=0.60, semantic=tangential, sources=2, diversity=0.50, contradiction=False
Supporting evidence document IDs: [2], [6]

Hypothese 2: Tunnel- und Portal-/Übergangsbereiche verursachen die stärkste Degradation der GNSS-Zugpositionierung: Im Tunnel kommt es zu vollständigem Signalausfall; direkt nach dem Portal entstehen durch Reacquisition unter Multipath/NLOS initial sehr große Fehler, die erst mit zunehmender Entfernung vom Portal wieder abklingen; im Modell liegt der mittlere Fehler im Tunnelszenario bei etwa 5.67 m (bei gleichzeitiger Phase ohne Positionierung im Tunnel).
Reasoning: fit=0.59, semantic=inferential, sources=2, diversity=0.50, contradiction=False
Supporting evidence document IDs: [11], [3], [5]

Hypothese 3: Bergiges Gelände verschlechtert GNSS-Genauigkeit im Bahnkorridor primär über deterministische Sichtbarkeitsselektion (Terrain-Occlusion): Satelliten werden aus der Positionslösung gefiltert, wenn ihr Elevationswinkel unter einem aus Berg-/Hindernishöhe und Distanz abgeleiteten Obstruktionswinkel liegt; dies erhöht den mittleren Positionsfehler gegenüber Open-Area messbar (z.B. von ≈1.03 m auf ≈1.30 m).
Reasoning: fit=0.55, semantic=tangential, sources=1, diversity=0.33, contradiction=False
Supporting evidence document IDs: [11], [2]

Hypothese 4: Die mathematische Bewertung der GNSS-Positionierungsqualität im Bahnkontext lässt sich operational über Integrity-Metriken (HPE, HPL, HAL, TTA sowie Pfa/Pmd) durchführen: Ein Alarm ist auszulösen, wenn HPL>HAL; Integritätszustände (Normal, SD, DD, DU, SU) ergeben sich aus den Ungleichungen zwischen HPE, HPL und HAL und erlauben eine sicherheitsgerichtete Klassifikation der Positionslösung über die reine Genauigkeit hinaus.
Reasoning: fit=0.53, semantic=tangential, sources=4, diversity=0.67, contradiction=False
Supporting evidence document IDs: [4], [14], [15], [16]

synthesis_output.summary:
## Kurzfazit
Verschiedene Signalstörungen (AM/FM/Puls-Interferenz, Jamming) und Gelände-/Infrastrukturprofile (Einschnitte/Cutting, bergige Abschattung, Tunnel inkl. Portalbereiche) verschlechtern die GNSS-Positionierungsgenauigkeit im Bahnkorridor über zwei Hauptmechanismen: (1) Messfehlerzuwachs in den GNSS-Beobachtungen (z. B. durch Interferenz/Jamming oder Multipath) und (2) Verschlechterung der Satellitengeometrie bzw. vollständiger Sichtbarkeitsverlust (z. B. durch Terrain-Masking oder Tunnelabschattung). Quantitativ zeigen die vorliegenden Studien Fehleranstiege von etwa ~1 m in offenen Bereichen auf mehrere Meter unter Interferenz und in Tunnel-/Übergangsbereichen; mathematisch lässt sich der Einfluss über Fehlermaße (Positionsfehler, Mittelwert/Standardabweichung, Fehlerellipse) sowie sicherheitsgerichtet über Integritätsmetriken (HPE/HPL/HAL, Pfa/Pmd, TTA und Zustandslogik) bewerten. [1][2][3][4][5]

## Relevante Störungsarten und ihre Wirkmechanismen
### In-band Interferenz (AM, FM, Puls)
Ein CPN/EKF-basierter Simulationsrahmen integriert explizit drei Interferenzarten (AM, FM, Puls) zur Bewertung der Positionierungsleistung in unterschiedlichen Umweltszenarien. [1][6]
- AM-Interferenz wirkt über Amplitudenvariationen und beeinflusst damit die Signalstärke; dies wird in den Ergebnissen besonders mit starken Schwankungen in der Höhen-/Elevationskomponente in Verbindung gebracht. [2]
- FM-Interferenz führt zu Frequenzverschiebungen und verstärkt Doppler-bezogene Effekte; sie erhöht die Fehlerfluktuationen vor allem in Ost-/Nordrichtung und zeigt insgesamt die stärkste Degradation. [2]
- Puls-Interferenz (periodische Bursts hoher Intensität) destabilisiert transient die Signalaufnahme und wirkt auf alle drei Raumdimensionen. [2]

### Intentionales oder unbeabsichtigtes Jamming
GNSS-Signale liegen typischerweise sehr schwach unterhalb des thermischen Rauschbodens (z. B. GPS-Signalpegel typischerweise um −127 dBm), weshalb Jamming das Tracking (Code-/Trägerschleifen) stören und dadurch die PVT-Lösung degradieren oder ausfallen lassen kann. [7][8]
Jamming wird u. a. anhand des Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR) bewertet; eine Studie fokussiert explizit ein 40 dB-JSR-Setup (−130 dBm GNSS-Signal, −90 dBm Jammer) und verweist darauf, dass bereits „intermediate“ Jamming-Level ohne vollständige Blockade eine signifikante, schwer detektierbare Degradation verursachen können. [9]
Jamming-Signale lassen sich nach Zeit-Frequenz-Verhalten u. a. in CW, AM, FM, Frequency Sweep (Chirp), Narrow-/Wideband Gaussian Noise klassifizieren; als in der Praxis verbreitet werden insbesondere Chirp-/Sweep-Signale genannt. [10]

## Einfluss spezifischer Gelände- und Infrastrukturprofile
### Bergiges Gelände / Terrain-Occlusion (Satellitensichtbarkeit)
Im CPN-Modell wird bergige Abschattung als deterministischer Sichtbarkeitsfilter modelliert: Aus Berg-/Hindernishöhe und Distanz wird ein Obstruktionswinkel abgeleitet und Satelliten werden aus der Positionslösung ausgeschlossen, wenn ihr Elevationswinkel unter diesem Obstruktionswinkel liegt. [11]
Dieses Vorgehen bildet mathematisch den Kernmechanismus von Terrain-Masking ab: weniger sichtbare Satelliten und veränderte Geometrie erhöhen typischerweise die Positionsunsicherheit (in den Ergebnissen wird für bergige Szenarien eine Verschlechterung gegenüber Open Area berichtet). [2][11]

### Einschnitt/Cutting (urbane Schlucht) und geometrische Degradation
Für eine „cutting environment“ (im Text auch als urban canyon eingeordnet) werden deutlich erhöhte horizontale Fehler berichtet. Die Autoren führen dies auf Abschattungen und Multipath zurück, die die Zahl sichtbarer Satelliten reduzieren und „bad geometries“ verursachen. [12]

### Tunnel und Portal-/Übergangsbereiche
Tunnel werden als Extremfall der Abschattung beschrieben:
- Im Tunnel selbst ist der GNSS-Empfang vollständig blockiert (keine Satellitensignale), wodurch die Positionierungsfähigkeit verloren geht. [11][3]
- Direkt nach dem Portal treten während der Signal-Reacquisition zunächst große Fehler auf, bedingt durch Multipath, Reflexionen und Verzögerungen nahe dem Tunnelexit; mit zunehmender Entfernung stabilisiert sich der Empfang und die Fehler nehmen ab. [11][3]
Dieser Übergangsmechanismus wird als kritischer Treiber der Degradation hervorgehoben. [3]

## Quantitative Befunde zu Genauigkeitsdegradation (Rail-Korridor)
### Fehleranstieg unter AM/FM/Puls-Interferenz (Simulation)
Unter Interferenzbedingungen steigen die Fehlerkennwerte von „präzisen“ Bedingungen auf mehrere Meter; dabei ist FM am stärksten. In den berichteten Simulationsergebnissen:
- Open-Area-Basis: mittlerer Fehler 1,0279 m (mit geringer Fluktuation) [2]
- AM-Interferenz: mittlerer Fehler 4,9484 m; Standardabweichung 4,0833 m [2]
- FM-Interferenz: mittlerer Fehler 6,2241 m; Standardabweichung 5,2630 m [2]
- Puls-Interferenz: mittlerer Fehler 4,7925 m; Standardabweichung 3,6242 m [2]

### Fehler in bergigem Gelände und Tunnel (Simulation)
Für Umweltszenarien werden ebenfalls Mittelwerte berichtet:
- Bergige Abschattung: mittlerer Fehler 1,2979 m (u. a. durch Multipath und Obstruktionen; Elevation besonders sensitiv) [2]
- Tunnel: mittlerer Fehler 5,6670 m; begründet mit starken Blockagen und Instabilität der Reacquisition nach dem Tunnel [3]

### Mess-/Testbasierte Fehler in Open Area vs. Cutting (Ray-Tracing/Zero on-site Testing)
In einer „zero on-site testing“-Plattform mit 3D-Digital-Track-Map und Ray-Tracing werden horizontale Fehlerstatistiken berichtet:
- Open Area: Mittelwert 0,75 m; Standardabweichung 0,12 m; Maximum 0,97 m [12]
- Cutting: Mittelwert 2,03 m; Standardabweichung 1,09 m; Maximum 9,58 m [12]
Zusätzlich wird explizit festgehalten, dass Mittelwert und Standardabweichung im Cutting etwa dreimal so groß wie in Open Area sind, was auf Abschattung/Multi­path und damit reduzierte Satellitenzahl sowie ungünstige Geometrie zurückgeführt wird. [12]

## Mathematische Bewertung: von Fehlermaßen bis Integritätsnachweis
### 1) Direktes Genauigkeitsmaß: Positionsfehler, Statistik und 3D-Abstand
Ein verwendetes Bewertungsmaß ist der euklidische 3D-Abstand zwischen Referenzposition und berechneter Position:
- d = ||(x1,y1,z1) − (x2,y2,z2)||, wobei (x1,y1,z1) die Referenz und (x2,y2,z2) die berechnete Position darstellt. [5]
Für Szenariovergleiche werden daraus statistische Kenngrößen (Mittelwert, Standardabweichung) abgeleitet und zwischen Umwelten bzw. Störarten verglichen. [2][3]

### 2) Fehlerellipse als 2D-Horizontalmetrik (inkl. Kovarianz)
Für horizontale Performance wird die Fehlerellipse genutzt; die Berechnung umfasst Standardabweichungen in x/y sowie Kovarianz und eine anschließende Standardisierung gemäß Fehlerellipsengleichung sowie Winkelbestimmung zwischen Ellipsenachse und x-Achse. [13]
Die Fehlerellipse dient damit als kompakte geometrische Darstellung von Streuung/Unsicherheit im Horizontalraum (z. B. über „long axis/short axis“). [12][13]

### 3) Integritätsmetriken und sicherheitsgerichtete Entscheidungslogik (Rail)
Für sicherheitsrelevante Bahn-Anwendungen werden Accuracy und Integrity als getrennte, aber gekoppelte Bewertungsdimensionen beschrieben. Accuracy wird über Positionsfehler e und einen Vertrauensgrad (1−α) gefasst. [4]
Integrity wird für horizontale Lösungen u. a. mit folgenden Parametern operationalisiert: HPE, Pfa, Pmd, HPL, HAL, TTA (Time to Alert). [4][14]
Eine konkrete Entscheidungslogik klassifiziert Integritätszustände über Ungleichungen zwischen HPE, HPL und HAL (Normal, DD, SD, DU, SU) und spezifiziert u. a.:
- Alarmbedingung: Wenn HPL > HAL, soll ein Alarm ausgelöst werden. [15]
- Zustandsklassen (Auszug): Normal, wenn HAL > HPL > HPE; Dangerous Undetected (DU), wenn HPE > HAL > HPL; Safe Undetected (SU), wenn HAL > HPE > HPL; sowie No-GNSS-Output als separater Fall. [16][15]
Diese Logik erlaubt eine mathematisch eindeutige, sicherheitsgerichtete Bewertung auch dann, wenn reine Fehlerstatistiken (Mittelwert/Std) die Risikolage (z. B. unentdeckte gefährliche Fehler) nicht ausreichend abbilden. [15][16]

### 4) Verknüpfung zu Availability/RAMS
Im Bahnkontext wird Availability im Sinne von EN50126 als „extent to which … available at any given moment“ beschrieben; als Eingangsgrößen werden u. a. Integritätsstatus, Hardware-Failure-Rate und Environmental-Scene-Parameter genannt. [17][4]
Tunnel-„Inside“-Phasen mit vollständiger Blockage sind damit nicht nur ein Genauigkeits-, sondern auch ein Availability-Problem (keine GNSS-Ausgabe). [11][3][17]

## Modellierungsansätze zur quantitativen Bewertung (wie man Gelände/Störung in Gleichungen bekommt)
### Deterministische Sichtbarkeitsmodelle (Terrain)
Der beschriebenen Mountain-Ansatz operationalisiert Abschattung über einen Obstruktionswinkel, der aus Höhen-/Distanzinformationen abgeleitet und mit dem Satelliten-Elevationswinkel verglichen wird; damit wird Sichtbarkeit (und indirekt Geometrie/Beobachtungsset) deterministisch selektiert. [11]

### 3D-Umweltmodell + Ray-Tracing (Multipath/NLOS)
Ray-Tracing wird als Kanalmodellierung eingesetzt, um LOS- und NLOS-Anteile deterministisch aus einer vorab modellierten 3D-Umgebung (z. B. LiDAR/UAV-3D-Modell) abzuleiten; Reflexionspfade werden u. a. über Spiegelpunktkonstruktion (auch 2. Ordnung) bestimmt. [13]
Die „zero on-site testing“-Architektur kombiniert digitale Track-Map, 3D-Umweltmodell und Ray-Tracing, um GNSS-Signale inkl. Multipath zu erzeugen und die Empfängerperformance zu evaluieren. [18][12]

### Störungssignal-Modelle (Interferenz/Jamming)
- In-band Interferenzen werden als explizite Signaltypen (AM/FM/Puls) als schaltbare Störmodule in die Beobachtungsdatengenerierung eingebracht. [6]
- Für Jamming wird ein Signalmodell am ADC-Ausgang angegeben, das LOS-GNSS-Signal, demoduliertes Jamming und additives weißes Gaußrauschen umfasst; darüber werden Klassifikation und Parametrisierung (z. B. Chirp/Sweep mit Start-/Endfrequenz und Sweep-Periode) formalisiert. [10]

## Schlussfolgerungen für die Forschungsfrage
1) Signalstörungen erhöhen GNSS-Positionsfehler primär über eine Verschlechterung der Beobachtungsqualität (Tracking-/Messfehler), wobei FM-Interferenz im betrachteten Vergleich die größten mittleren Fehler und Streuungen erzeugt. [2]
2) Geländeprofile wirken über Sichtbarkeits- und Geometriemechanismen: Terrain-Masking filtert Satelliten (Elevations- vs. Obstruktionswinkel), Einschnitte reduzieren Sichtbarkeit und verschlechtern die Geometrie; Tunnel führen zu totalem Signalausfall und kritischen Reacquisition-Fehlern am Portal. [11][12][3]
3) Mathematisch lässt sich der Einfluss mehrstufig bewerten: (a) metrisch über 3D-Euklidfehler und dessen Statistik, (b) horizontal über Fehlerellipse/Kovarianz, und (c) sicherheitsgerichtet über Integritätsmetriken (HPE/HPL/HAL, Pfa/Pmd, TTA) und Zustandslogik inklusive Alarmbedingungen. [5][13][4][15][16]

- Parametrisierung/Leistungskennwerte für Spoofing (gezielte Täuschung) fehlen vollständig in den bereitgestellten Quellen.
